hints: The $\Delta$ means "change in". It always comes before the variable we want to track the change in. For example, $\Delta m$ means "change in the mass". When we say "rate of change", we mean change in a quantity divided by change in time, by default. For example, if you're pouring water into a cup, the mass of water in the cup is increasing. The rate of change of the mass in the cup would be $\dfrac{\Delta m}{\Delta t}.$

\\nCreate equations for the displacement, velocity, and acceleration of the object. Each should use at least one
\\n\"}"}]
solution_text:
The $\Delta$ means "change in". It always comes before the variable we want to track the change in. For example, $\Delta m$ means "change in the mass". When we say "rate of change", we mean change in a quantity divided by change in time, by default. For example, if you're pouring water into a cup, the mass of water in the cup is increasing. The rate of change of the mass in the cup would be $\dfrac{\Delta m}{\Delta t}.$

The equations are
[center][img]http://cdn.artofproblemsolving.com/school/crypt/00405-36293f6558d60af76e24b26cd4d75d9753687fc0/files/02_hw_equations_answer.jpg[/img][/center]
These are the same as the definitions given in class over the last two weeks, but we have replaced the more typical coordinate $x$ with the coordinate $z.$ In physics, it's useful to be flexible in how you use notation. Different sources and different sub-fields of physics use notation differently, so we need to be able to adapt to those small differences.

These equations can be read as saying,

"Displacement is a change in something's position (or its coordinate)."

"Velocity is the rate of change of displacement."

"Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity."
